serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of tagliolini with cream and mushrooms is approximately 320 g, comprising a portion of cooked pasta dressed with mushroom cream sauce and Parmesan.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 20420) combined with component estimation for cream sauce and mushrooms
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for tagliolini with cream and mushroom sauce as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted-component method: approximately 55% cooked tagliolini (USDA SR Legacy 20420), 25% heavy cream (USDA SR Legacy 01053), 15% mixed mushrooms cooked (USDA SR Legacy 11260), and 5% Parmesan cheese (USDA SR Legacy 01032). Macros and secondary nutrients are derived from these proportions and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods entries for pasta dishes with cream sauce.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; pasta (white, boiled) GI reference values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tagliolini with cream and mushroom sauce. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on white pasta (boiled, al dente) values from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 32–52 for white pasta, reference food glucose = 100). The cream and mushroom sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load relative to the pasta base. A midpoint value of 45 is used, consistent with Atkinson et al. (2021) pasta category. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 21.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 320 g serving) / 100 = 31.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and saturated fat from cream and Parmesan
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 185 mg sodium per 100 g (592 mg per standard 320 g serving), individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation; the saturated fat from cream also contributes to vascular load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 31 per serving and a GI of 45, this dish has a moderate overall glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, prefer al dente cooking to lower GI further, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from heavy cream and Parmesan cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 6.1 g saturated fat and 38 mg cholesterol per 100 g, primarily from heavy cream and Parmesan; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider requesting lighter cream or reduced-fat alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tagliolini with Cream and Mushrooms (Italian: Tagliolini Panna e Funghi) is a classic Northern Italian pasta dish featuring tagliolini — a very thin, ribbon-like egg pasta similar to tagliatelle but narrower — tossed in a rich sauce made from fresh heavy cream, sautéed mixed mushrooms (such as porcini, cremini, or champignon), freshly grated Parmesan cheese, Italian flat-leaf parsley, and cracked black pepper. The pasta is typically handmade and cooked al dente, giving it a tender yet slightly firm bite. The cream sauce is silky and aromatic, with the earthy umami depth of mushrooms balanced by the saltiness of Parmesan. This dish is a staple of Italian trattoria menus and is served as a primo piatto (first course). Nutritionally, it is energy-dense due to the cream and cheese, providing approximately 195 kcal per 100 g with moderate protein (5.8 g), significant fat (10.2 g, predominantly saturated), and refined carbohydrates (21.5 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it moderately suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in controlled portions. Clinically, the dish warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension (sodium ~185 mg/100 g), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat ~6.1 g/100 g), and Type 2 Diabetes (glycemic load ~31 per serving). It contains gluten, dairy, and egg allergens and is suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians but not vegans.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for tagliolini with cream and mushroom sauce as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta, heavy cream, cooked mushrooms, and Parmesan cheese. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database pasta values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: cooked enriched pasta (USDA 20420), heavy cream (USDA 01053), cooked mushrooms (USDA 11260), and Parmesan cheese (USDA 01032), weighted approximately 55:25:15:5. 2. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on white pasta (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr); GI range for white pasta is 32–52 with midpoint 45 used. 3. Serving size of 320 g is estimated from typical Italian restaurant pasta portion norms; no direct restaurant weight data available. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the same component-weighted USDA sources; no single composite dish entry was available. 5. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese cuisines do not apply. Chinese names are provided as the dish is served in Singapore and Chinese-language alias coverage supports local retrieval. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable for an Italian dish and are left null. 6. The restaurant source (Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, field survey data) is a forbidden source for nutrient values and was not used for nutritional data. 7. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrient fields with numeric values are estimated from approved USDA component sources as documented above.
